title: Scaling Behaviors of Work Cumulants in Slow Isothermal Processes
authors: Ruohan Xu, Yanbo Qiao, H. T. Quan
abstract: We study the cumulants of work in a slow isothermal process for gapped systems. Using the Martin-Siggia-Rose-De Dominicis-Janssen (MSRDJ) formalism and the properties of connected correlation functions, we show that in this process, the $n$-th cumulant of work scales as $1/T^{n-1}$ , where $T$ is the time duration. This result holds generally for arbitrary smooth protocols. Furthermore, we derive the coefficients of the cumulants from equilibrium properties. These coefficients are found to be relevant to thermodynamic geometric tensors.
summary: This work derives the universal power-law scaling for higher-order work cumulants ($\kappa_n \propto T^{-(n-1)}$) during slow isothermal processes in gapped quantum systems. Using advanced field theory techniques, it shows these coefficients are determined by equilibrium geometric properties. This establishes a powerful connection between non-equilibrium fluctuations and static thermodynamic geometry.
main_problem: The study focuses on determining the scaling behaviors of work cumulants during slow isothermal processes in gapped quantum systems.
main_result: The $n$-th cumulant of work ($\kappa_n$) scales universally as $\kappa_n \propto (1/T)^{n-1}$ for large time durations $T$, with coefficients related to equilibrium thermodynamic geometric tensors.
method: The analysis employs the Martin-Siggia-Rose-De Dominicis-Janssen (MSRDJ) field theory formalism, utilizing connected correlation functions and perturbation theory.
model_or_system: The system consists of gapped quantum systems driven slowly by time-dependent potentials, modeled via a Langevin equation. The analysis is general for arbitrary smooth protocols but confirmed with examples like the breathing oscillator.
key_observables: Work cumulants ($\kappa_n$), excess work ($\kappa_2$), and thermodynamic geometric tensors ($K_n$).
important_parameters: Slow driving time duration $T$, gap size (required for convergence), and protocol parameters $\lambda(t)$.
main_assumptions: The derivation fundamentally relies on the existence of a finite mass gap; the scaling breaks down near continuous phase transitions.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares exact results with asymptotic predictions for the work cumulants in the slow-driving limit, showing agreement.
paper_structure: The paper builds from established knowledge regarding excess work ($\kappa_2$) to generalize the scaling law for all $\kappa_n$. It first analyzes a simple linear driving case before generalizing the result using MSRDJ theory for arbitrary smooth protocols.
why_interesting: This provides rigorous, general results on non-equilibrium thermodynamics far from equilibrium, connecting fluctuation theorems (work cumulants) to underlying geometric structures (thermodynamic tensors) in the slow limit.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Comparison between exact (solid) and asymptotic (dashed) results of case 2. The parameters can be found in the first paragraph of Sec. IV
